title: Yapp binding
subtitle: The projecting wallet-edge flexible binding used for Bibles, prayer books, and field notebooks
excerpt: A yapp binding has a cover that extends beyond the text block on the foredge and often at head and tail, forming a wallet-like projection that protects the pages from the outside edge. The cover is flexible, limp leather or imitation leather rather than rigid board. Used for devotional books from the Victorian period onward.
sourceNotes: Cyril Davenport, The Book, Its History and Development (1907). Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on flexible and limp covers. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock.

glossaryTerms.definition: The portion of the cover that projects beyond the text block on the foredge and, in full yapp bindings, at head and tail. Named after the London bookseller William Yapp who popularised the style in the 1860s.
A book cover made from soft, flexible material without rigid boards. The cover wraps the text block directly. Common in devotional books, field manuals, and medieval manuscripts.
The outer edge of a book's pages, opposite the spine.

content.text: , no boards, so the leather wraps around the sewn text block and overlaps the
. The projecting edge protects page corners from knocks and lets the book fit in a coat pocket without the page edges being damaged.

The text block: sew and consolidate as for any case binding. Apply a coat of PVA to the spine and glue on the book mull, leaving 20 mm wings on each side. Trim the text block to size with the foredge flush and square. Add endpapers.
Leather cut dimensions: spine height + 12 mm (6 mm overhang at head and tail) × cover width + spine width + cover width + 20 mm (10 mm yapp overhang at each foredge).

Cut the leather to the calculated dimensions. Mark the spine position on the flesh side of the leather with a pencil: two lines, each at the text block cover width from the foredge edges, spanning the spine width between them.

Score the spine lines lightly with the bone folder to create hinge points. Do not cut through the leather, just compress the fibre at the hinge. The cover must fold at the spine-to-board transition, and the scored hinge makes this clean.
Apply diluted PVA to the spine portion of the leather's flesh side. Lay the text block spine onto it and press. Wrap the leather around the text block, folding the hinges along the scored lines.
Apply PVA to the leather's inner faces where they meet the mull wings. Press the mull wings flat against the leather inner surface.
Paste the endpapers to the leather inner faces. The endpaper should stop 5-8 mm from the foredge, leaving the yapp projection free of adhesive so it can curve naturally.

Press under board weight for two hours. The yapp projection should curve slightly inward when the book is closed, this is correct. Do not press the projecting edges flat; let them find their natural curve.

items.symptom: The yapp projections flare outward rather than curling gently inward
items.cause: The leather is too thick or stiff for the overhang width.
items.fix: Use 1.0-1.2 mm leather for yapp work. Skive the foredge strip to 0.6 mm for the last 15 mm. Moisten the projections and hold them curved inward for five minutes while the leather readjusts.

items.symptom: The spine cracks or stiffens after drying
items.cause: Too much PVA was applied to the spine leather.
items.fix: Use diluted PVA (1 part PVA to 1 part water) for the spine joint. Full-strength PVA stiffens thin leather as it dries and can cause cracking after repeated opening.
